---
description: "One-sentence, reader-facing promise: the concrete thing this chapter builds or measures."
---

# Exact Title {#sec-chNN}

Open on the concrete artifact in the first sentence: name the thing this chapter
builds or measures, then enumerate its parts inline. Anchor to earlier chapters
with an inline reference ("recall from @sec-ch09 that...") rather than a
prerequisites block. One to three short paragraphs, no headings, no scaffolding.

## First teaching section

Motivate the idea in plain prose before any formal object: say what breaks
without this mechanism, name the fix conversationally, and only then reach for
the equation or spec. Introduce the reusable helper the rest of the chapter
extends. The cell below carries `# @save`, so `scripts/tangle.py` exports it to
`code/chNN/_generated.py` for the tests to import.

```{python}
# @save
from __future__ import annotations

import math


def softmax(logits: list[float], temperature: float) -> list[float]:
    """Turn raw scores into a probability distribution over next tokens.

    Temperature rescales the logits before exponentiation: values below 1
    sharpen the distribution toward the argmax, values above 1 flatten it.

    Args:
        logits: Unnormalized scores, one per candidate token.
        temperature: Positive scale; smaller is greedier, larger is flatter.

    Returns:
        Probabilities in the same order as ``logits``, summing to 1.
    """
    scaled = [value / temperature for value in logits]
    ceiling = max(scaled)
    weights = [math.exp(value - ceiling) for value in scaled]
    total = sum(weights)
    return [weight / total for weight in weights]


logits = [2.0, 1.0, 0.2]
print([round(p, 3) for p in softmax(logits, 1.0)])
```

Interpret the printed distribution before moving on: name which token dominates
and why. Then show the same idea as a picture the reader can scan at a glance.

```{python}
#| label: fig-chNN-softmax
#| fig-cap: "How does temperature reshape one next-token distribution?"
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

for temperature in (0.5, 1.0, 2.0):
    plt.plot(softmax(logits, temperature), marker="o", label=f"T={temperature}")
plt.xlabel("token")
plt.ylabel("probability")
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

Read @fig-chNN-softmax honestly: at low temperature the top token takes almost
all the mass; at high temperature the curve flattens.

## Second teaching section

State why the next step follows. Build one more fragment on the helper above, run
it, and show what it produces. Every runnable cell shows its result.

```{python}
#| label: fig-chNN-entropy
#| fig-cap: "How fast does output entropy climb with temperature?"
temperatures = [0.25 * step for step in range(1, 13)]
entropy = [-sum(p * math.log2(p) for p in softmax(logits, t) if p > 0) for t in temperatures]
plt.plot(temperatures, entropy, marker="o")
plt.xlabel("temperature")
plt.ylabel("entropy (bits)")
plt.show()
```

Name the regime where entropy saturates (@fig-chNN-entropy) and what that implies
for sampling.

## Third teaching section

Continue the same beat for every idea. A real chapter has five to nine sections;
the single integrated build grows across them, so by the last section the
artifact exists, has run, and its measurements are on the page. Reserve a concept
diagram for the moments prose cannot carry a flow.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-chNN-flow
%%| fig-cap: "Where does the mechanism sit in the larger system?"
flowchart LR
    Input --> Mechanism --> Result
```

State the one conclusion the diagram carries, then move on.

## Summary

Sixty to a hundred and ten words that synthesize what was built and measured, not
a step-by-step recap. Lead with the capability the reader gained, name one honest
limitation, and leave a single forward pointer to the next chapter.

## Exercises

1. Confirm one mechanism by hand and check it against the printed output.
2. Change one knob in the cell above and predict the result before re-running.
3. Break one invariant and diagnose the trace it produces.
4. Extend the chapter's artifact without rewriting it, then re-measure.
5. Compare one alternative design through a measurement, not an assertion.
6. A multi-part item: (a) vary a second knob; (b) plot the result; (c) explain
   the trend you see.
